title: ION Words and Verbs - Quiero
description: Words ending in -ION are the same in both languages. Introduction to 'quiero' (I want) and creating verbs from -ción nouns.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: 'I love you' in Spanish is 'te quiero'. Literally, it means 'I want you'. You might have also heard 'te amo'. Te quiero you can use with friends, with lovers early on. Te amo is much more serious.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So in 'te quiero', 'te' is 'you'. We can get rid of that. What are we left with for 'I want'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'I don't want'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No quiero. Good. We just put 'no' before it.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: One of my favorite rules for convertible words is the rule for words ending I-O-N. This is extremely useful. Words ending I-O-N are the same in English and Spanish, just in Spanish they have the accent on the end.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So in English we have 'opinion'. In Spanish you pronounce syllable by syllable, making sure you pronounce the vowels as written, and put the accent on the end. How would you say opinion?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Pasión. Good. And you pronounce the S, not like 'sh'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now if it's a 'shun' sound - T-I-O-N - it becomes C-I-Ó-N in Spanish. This is pronounced 'sion' in most of the Spanish speaking world, or 'thion' in most of Spain.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Confirmación. Good. Remember, I is like 'ink', not like in English 'confirmation'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: This is what we call language transfer. Negative language transfer is when things from English affect your Spanish incorrectly. Positive language transfer is using what's helpful from English - like these convertible words.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Conversación. Good. 'Con' means 'with' - it's like 'with verses', with versus.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Complicación. Good. Here 'con' also becomes 'com' because of that P - it happens the same in English and Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now the reason this is my favorite rule: if you take words ending in -CIÓN and remove the -ción, then put an R on the end, you get the verb!

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So preparation - preparación. What is 'to prepare'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Preparar. Good. So now we know about a thousand verbs in Spanish!

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Confirmar. Good. If 'to confirm' is 'with signing', how do you think you would say 'to sign'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now we can make sentences. What was 'I want' again? If you forget, think of 'I love you' in Spanish and take away the 'you'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I want to prepare'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Cooperación. Good. You pronounce both O's - it sounds a bit longer.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I want to cooperate'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I want to cancel'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'I don't want to cancel'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I want to explore'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: The word for 'me' in Spanish is the same as English - you just pronounce it how it's written: me.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you say 'I want to prepare myself'? In Spanish you say 'I want to prepare me'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Quiero prepararme. Good. This means 'I want to get ready'. You can use this like that: quiero prepararme.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: This probably sounds quite formal in English - 'I want to prepare myself'. But in Spanish, Latin words don't sound formal. They just sound normal. That's because Spanish is modern Latin!
